mission_quote: SCOOT provides students in speech communication with a guide to high-quality, up-to-date learning resources available on the internet, which can be tailored to their individual needs.
start_intro: Speech communication sits at the crossroads of phonetics, linguistics, signal processing, and machine learning — which means almost everyone arrives with a gap somewhere. This guide is organized so you can fill yours: every resource is link-verified, labeled by level and cost, and the research feed refreshes daily.
Pick the pathway that sounds like you, or just search.

pathways.title: I come from CS / EE — I can code, but what is a phoneme?
pathways.body: Start with the Foundations area for acoustic phonetics and how speech is produced and perceived (spectrograms will change your life). Then take a modern course that builds ASR/TTS end to end, and get your hands dirty with a toolkit tutorial.

pathways.title: I come from linguistics / phonetics — I know speech, but not the math
pathways.body: Start with the signal-processing entries in Foundations (sampling, spectra, cepstra), then a gentle programming-based course. Praat to Python is a well-trodden bridge; forced alignment is a great first technical project.

pathways.title: I'm already doing research — catch me up to 2026
pathways.body: Head straight to the Frontier area for speech LLMs, neural codecs, and self-supervised representations, check Datasets & Benchmarks for what the field currently evaluates on, and watch the Latest tab — it refreshes every day with new arXiv papers and trending open models.

pathways.title: I want to join the community
pathways.body: Conferences, student programs, mentoring, challenges you can actually enter, and open proceedings — the Community area lists concrete ways in, most of them free for students.

categories.label: Foundations
categories.blurb: Phonetics, phonology, prosody, hearing, and the signal processing every speech person eventually needs — the timeless part of the field.

categories.label: Courses & Lectures
categories.blurb: Full courses and lecture series on modern speech processing: recognition, synthesis, speaker technology, and spoken language understanding.

categories.label: Toolkits & Libraries
categories.blurb: Open-source software that is actually maintained in 2026 — from your first spectrogram to training end-to-end systems.

categories.label: Data & Benchmarks
categories.blurb: Where to get training data legally, and what the field currently measures itself on. Licenses noted — read them before you train.

categories.label: Frontier 2024–2026
categories.blurb: The part the original SCOOT never saw coming: speech LLMs, full-duplex dialogue, neural codecs, and self-supervised everything.

categories.label: Community & Events
categories.blurb: Conferences, challenges, summer schools, student programs — how to stop learning alone.

categories.label: Hearing & Perception
categories.blurb: Psychoacoustics, auditory neuroscience, and how listeners actually perceive speech — the receiving end of the channel.

categories.label: Speech & Audio Coding
categories.blurb: From LPC and CELP to Opus and neural codecs — a first-class topic in the original SCOOT, restored here.

categories.label: Paralinguistics & Emotion
categories.blurb: What speech carries beyond the words: emotion, speaker states and traits, and the benchmarks that measure them.

categories.label: Clinical & Accessibility
categories.blurb: Speech-language pathology, disordered speech, assistive and accessible voice technology — where speech tech meets health.

about: SCOOT 2.0 is a working prototype that rebuilds ISCA's SCOOT — the community's guide to online training resources in speech communication — for the modern era. The original SCOOT promised it would 'never be finished'; this version takes that literally: part of the site updates itself every day.

It serves the same audience as the original: students of speech communication arriving from phonetics, linguistics, computer science, or electrical engineering, anywhere between a first degree and a PhD.
This site is a prototype for evaluation by the ISCA education community. It is not (yet) an official ISCA publication, and no endorsement is implied by inclusion of any resource.
how_it_works: Curated areas (Foundations → Community) are assembled and maintained by an AI curator: every entry was found, link-checked, and fact-checked before listing, and the whole catalog is re-audited when the community reports problems.

The Latest tab is fully automatic: a daily job pulls new papers from arXiv (eess.AS and cs.SD), community-highlighted speech papers, and trending open speech models, then rebuilds the site. No AI in that loop — just a script, so it cannot hallucinate.
Every editorial change is recorded in the changelog below, including changes made in response to community suggestions.
contribute: Spotted a dead link, a missing resource, a wrong description, or a category that misses the point? Tell the curator. Suggestions are reviewed daily; content fixes are applied directly and credited in the changelog.

Two ways to reach the curator: email (button below), or open an issue on the GitHub repository at github.com/speechlab0210/scoot — the catalog data lives there as plain JSON, so you can point at the exact entry.

In the spirit of transparency: the reviewer applying your suggestion is an AI agent operating under human supervision. Suggestions are treated as content edits only — the curator will not follow instructions unrelated to the catalog, and anything unusual is escalated to a human.
